title: TypeScript Modules and Namespaces
summary: ES module syntax, CommonJS interop, namespaces, ambient declarations, and module resolution strategies.

questions.prompt: A TypeScript file becomes a module (not a global script) when:
choices: It has the .ts extension
It contains at least one import or export statement
It is in the src/ folder
It has a named export
questions.explanation: The presence of `import` or `export` makes a file a module with its own scope. Without either, the file is a global script — all its top-level declarations are added to the global scope and can conflict with other files.

questions.prompt: What does `import type { User } from './types'` do differently from `import { User } from './types'`?
choices: No difference — TypeScript erases both at compile time
type-only import is completely erased from the JavaScript output — no runtime import statement is emitted
type-only imports are faster at runtime
type imports are only allowed in .d.ts files
questions.explanation: `import type` is erased entirely — no `require()` or `import()` call remains in the JS output. This avoids circular dependency issues, reduces bundle size, and prevents importing a value as if it were a type (a runtime error). Regular `import` emits the import statement even for type-only uses.

questions.prompt: How would you import ALL exports from `./utils` under a single object named `utils`?
choices: A — default import
B — namespace import
C — named import
D — CommonJS import
questions.explanation: `import * as utils from './utils'` is a namespace import — all named exports become properties of `utils`. `utils.add()`, `utils.PI` etc. Option A gets a default export. C gets one named export called 'utils'. D is CJS-style.

questions.prompt: What is an ambient declaration (`declare const x: string`)?
choices: A variable declaration that TypeScript will create at runtime
Tells TypeScript that a value already exists at runtime (from an external source) without TypeScript generating any code for it
A type alias
A deprecated feature replaced by interface
questions.explanation: `declare` says 'this exists at runtime, I'm just describing its type'. No JavaScript is emitted. Used for: global variables injected by the HTML (`declare const ENV: string`), external scripts, augmenting module types (`.d.ts` files).

questions.prompt: After adding a path alias `'@/*': ['src/*']` in tsconfig, you can immediately use `import { x } from '@/utils'` in:
choices: Both TypeScript type checking AND the bundler (Vite/webpack)
TypeScript type checking only — the bundler needs its own alias configuration
The bundler only — TypeScript ignores paths
Neither — paths aliases require tsc compilation mode
questions.explanation: `tsconfig paths` is for TypeScript's language server and type checking ONLY. The bundler (Vite, webpack) does not read tsconfig paths. You must configure an identical alias in `vite.config.ts` (resolve.alias) or webpack.config (resolve.alias).

questions.prompt: What is `esModuleInterop: true` needed for?
choices: Enabling ES modules in TypeScript
Allowing clean default imports from CommonJS modules like `import React from 'react'` instead of `import * as React`
Enabling top-level await
Converting ESM to CJS
questions.explanation: Without `esModuleInterop`, CJS modules that use `module.exports = X` must be imported as `import * as X`. With it, TypeScript synthesizes a default export, allowing `import X from 'module'`. Most modern tsconfigs have this enabled.

questions.prompt: A `declare module '*.svg'` ambient declaration in a `types.d.ts` file allows:
choices: TypeScript to compile SVG files to JavaScript
TypeScript to type SVG imports — without it, `import logo from './logo.svg'` would be a type error
SVG animations
SVG optimization at build time
questions.explanation: TypeScript doesn't natively know the type of `.svg` imports. An ambient module declaration `declare module '*.svg' { ... }` teaches TypeScript what type to assign. Vite/webpack handle the actual transformation at runtime — TypeScript just needs the type info.

questions.prompt: Which statements about namespaces are true?
choices: Namespaces compile to IIFEs with a shared object
Modern TypeScript prefers ES modules over namespaces
Namespaces and ES modules can be freely mixed in the same project
Namespaces are primarily used in .d.ts files for organizing global types
questions.explanation: Namespaces DO compile to IIFEs. Modern code prefers ES modules. Namespaces are still used in `.d.ts` files to organize types (e.g., `declare namespace NodeJS { interface ProcessEnv {...} }`). Mixing namespaces and ES modules in the same file is not recommended — TypeScript may behave unexpectedly.

questions.prompt: What does TypeScript look for when resolving `import { add } from './math'`?
questions.code:
// Module resolution (node strategy) tries files in order:
// Which sequence is correct?
choices: ./math.js, ./math.ts, ./math.d.ts
./math.ts, ./math.tsx, ./math/index.ts, ./math.d.ts
./math.ts only
./math/math.ts
questions.explanation: Node module resolution tries: `./math.ts`, `./math.tsx`, `./math.d.ts`, `./math/index.ts`, `./math/index.tsx`, `./math/index.d.ts`. The `.js` file is checked in `node16` resolution mode. Extensions can be explicit or inferred.

questions.prompt: What does `export * as utils from './utils'` do?
choices: Imports all from utils into a local 'utils' variable
Re-exports all named exports from./utils as a namespace named 'utils' — importers get `import { utils } from './index'; utils.add()`
Exports only the default from ./utils
Error — you cannot alias re-exports
questions.explanation: `export * as utils` is a namespace re-export. Consumers: `import { utils } from './index'` then `utils.add()`. It's a convenient way to expose an entire module under a nested namespace without an intermediate import.

questions.prompt: How do you augment Express's `Request` type to include a `user` property without editing node_modules?
choices: Create a new interface called Request
Create a .d.ts file with `declare module 'express' { interface Request { user?: User } }` — declaration merges into Express's existing Request interface
Import Express and extend its class
Use a type assertion everywhere: `(req as any).user`
questions.explanation: Module augmentation: `declare module 'express' { interface Request { user?: User } }` merges your declaration into Express's existing `Request` interface. TypeScript now understands `req.user` in all routes. Zero runtime cost — pure type annotation.

questions.prompt: Circular imports (A imports B, B imports A) are problematic in CJS but less so in ESM. Why?
choices: ESM is newer and fixes the bug
CJS resolves modules eagerly and may see 'undefined' for values that haven't been assigned yet. ESM uses live bindings — references resolve to current values at call time
Circular imports are invalid TypeScript
CJS doesn't support default exports
questions.explanation: In CJS, if A imports B and B imports A, by the time B's `require('./a')` runs, A's module may only be partially executed — some exports are `undefined`. ESM live bindings point to the binding cell, which gets filled in when the module finishes. Circular deps still require care but are less dangerous.

questions.prompt: A file says `const x = 5;` with no imports or exports. In TypeScript this is:
choices: A module — const defines a module-scoped variable
A global script — x is added to the global scope and conflicts with same-named vars in other files
A declaration file
An error — all TypeScript files must have exports
questions.explanation: No `import` or `export` = global script. `x` becomes a global variable. If another file also declares `const x = 5`, TypeScript errors on duplicate identifier. Fix: add `export {}` at the bottom to make it a module (even without exporting anything).

questions.prompt: What is a `.d.ts` file and what does it contain?
choices: A compiled JavaScript file
A TypeScript declaration file — contains only type information (interfaces, type aliases, declare statements) with zero JavaScript output
A deprecated TypeScript format
A configuration file
questions.explanation: `.d.ts` files are pure type declaration files. They describe the types of a library or codebase without containing any executable code. TypeScript uses them for type checking. When you install `@types/lodash`, you get `.d.ts` files that describe lodash's API.

questions.prompt: Which are valid reasons to use `import type`?
choices: Prevent circular import issues caused by value imports
Ensure a symbol is completely removed from the JS bundle if it's only used as a type
Improve IDE autocomplete performance
Avoid accidentally using a type as a value
questions.explanation: `import type` helps with circular deps (no runtime import), ensures the import is fully erased from output (bundle optimization), and prevents accidentally calling a type as a function. IDE performance is not a primary reason.

questions.prompt: What happens when you `export default` a function and a colleague imports it as `import MyFn from './fn'`?
choices: Error — default imports must use the original function name
Works — the name 'MyFn' is the importer's choice; default exports carry no required name
Works — but TypeScript warns about name mismatch
Only works if the function has the same name as the file
questions.explanation: Default exports can be imported with any name the importer chooses. This is by design — they represent 'the main thing this module exports'. The downside: inconsistent naming across a codebase. Many style guides prefer named exports for this reason.

questions.prompt: tsconfig `moduleResolution: 'bundler'` is recommended when:
choices: Using tsc directly to compile to Node.js
Using Vite, webpack, or another bundler — it matches the real resolution algorithm used by the bundler, allowing extensionless imports and .tsx files
Writing library code published to npm
Using Deno
questions.explanation: `moduleResolution: 'bundler'` matches how Vite/webpack resolve modules — it allows importing without extensions and supports `.tsx`. For Node.js libraries use `node16` or `nodenext`. For Deno use `node16` with specific adjustments.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of `export {}` at the bottom of a TypeScript file?
choices: Exports all variables
Makes the file a module (enables module scope) without exporting anything — prevents variables from leaking to global scope
Clears all exports
Marks the file as ambient
questions.explanation: Empty `export {}` is a common trick: it makes a file a module (adding module scope isolation) without exporting anything. Useful for files that define globals or side effects but shouldn't have exports.

questions.prompt: Which of these correctly describe barrel files (`index.ts`)?
choices: Re-export multiple modules so consumers import from one location
Can cause large bundle sizes if tree-shaking doesn't work correctly
Required by TypeScript for directories
Can create circular dependency issues if modules within the directory import from the barrel
questions.explanation: Barrel files re-export for convenience: `export { Button } from './Button'; export { Input } from './Input'`. This helps DX but can bloat bundles if tree-shaking fails. Modules inside the same directory importing the barrel (instead of each other directly) can create circular deps.

questions.prompt: You install a package `foo` with no TypeScript types. The error is: 'Could not find a declaration file for module foo'. The cleanest fix is:
choices: Add `// @ts-ignore` to every import
Install `@types/foo` from DefinitelyTyped, OR create a `foo.d.ts` under `src/types/` with `declare module 'foo'`
Convert the package to TypeScript
Use `require('foo')` instead
questions.explanation: If `@types/foo` exists on npm, install it. If not, create a stub: `declare module 'foo' { const x: any; export default x; }`. For better types, write fuller declarations in your own `.d.ts`. `@ts-ignore` hides the error everywhere but gives you no type safety.

flashcards.back: Any `import` or `export` = module (scoped). No `import`/`export` = global script (pollutes global scope). Use `export {}` to force module scope without exporting.
`import type { T }` is fully erased — no runtime import. Use it for types to avoid circular deps and reduce bundle. Regular `import` emits a runtime statement.
paths aliases work for TypeScript type checking ONLY. Bundlers (Vite, webpack) need their own alias config. Both configs must point to the same directories.
`declare const x: T` — tells TypeScript about a value that exists at runtime but wasn't written in TypeScript. Emits zero JavaScript. Used in `.d.ts` files and for injected globals.
`declare module 'express' { interface Request { user?: User } }` — merges into Express's existing types. Lets you add fields to library interfaces without touching node_modules.
Enables clean `import React from 'react'` for CJS packages. Without it: `import * as React from 'react'`. Most modern tsconfigs set it to true.
Compiles to an IIFE with a shared object. Use in .d.ts for organizing global types. Modern code uses ES modules instead. Namespaces inside ES module files are discouraged.
index.ts re-exports from multiple files — convenient for importers. Risk: can create circular deps inside the folder; can defeat tree-shaking if bundler can't analyse re-exports.

project.brief: Your application has grown to have deep relative imports like `import { UserService } from '../../../services/UserService'`. Design a clean module structure: 1) Propose folder structure and path aliases that eliminate deep relative imports. 2) Write the tsconfig paths config AND the Vite resolve.alias config that implements it. 3) Create a barrel `index.ts` for a `services/` folder — list 3 potential circular dependency risks and how to avoid them. 4) You need to type an untyped npm package `color-picker` that exports `{ pickColor(): string }`. Write the ambient declaration. 5) Extend Express's Request type to include `currentUser: { id: string; role: string } | undefined`.

checklist.text: tsconfig paths and Vite alias configs are consistent and point to same directories
3 circular dep risks identified: self-import, cross-service import through barrel, barrel importing from consumer
Ambient declaration for color-picker written as declare module with correct shape
Express Request augmentation uses declare module 'express' + interface Request

hints: Circular risk: ServiceA imports ServiceB, ServiceB imports index.ts which re-exports ServiceA → cycle.
Ambient: `declare module 'color-picker' { export function pickColor(): string; }`
Express augment: in a `src/types/express.d.ts` — `declare module 'express' { interface Request { currentUser?: {...} } }`
